answer: Argon2id with parameters 64MB memory, 3 iterations, and parallelism 4.
Refresh tokens expire in 7 days and use rotate-on-use rotation.
PostgreSQL 15.4, upgraded from 14.9.
API Gateway throttle is 5000 burst and 2000 steady.
Argon2id was chosen because it provides better side-channel resistance than bcrypt.
Drizzle is used because it has zero runtime dependencies and a SQL-like TypeScript experience.
Both services validate JWTs via JWKS (RS256), consistent with the all-services-auth rule; gateway Lambda authorizer also validates via JWKS.
Rate limiting is done at ALB to stop DDoS traffic before it reaches containers, which app-level limiting cannot do as effectively.

Request path: API Gateway schema validation -> Lambda authorizer validates JWT via JWKS and injects claims -> VPC Link -> ALB (rate limit) -> Portfolio Service, which also validates via JWKS and applies RBAC middleware/role hierarchy before allowing rebalance.

If JWKS is down, JWT validation fails across downstream auth checks (gateway Lambda and services). Token issuance continues because the private signing key is local. There is an LRU cache (1000 entries, 5-minute TTL) that provides short grace behavior for cached keys.
Real-time portfolio alerting is planned for Q3 and depends on both notify-service and a gateway WebSocket API.
Yes. The UTC+13/+14 expiry bug is marked resolved by switching expiry handling to Unix timestamps instead of Date objects.

Use the existing webhook pattern in notify-service: enterprise registers endpoint via POST /api/v1/notifications/webhooks/register, auth events are emitted to notify-service, and deliveries use exponential backoff with 5 attempts over 24 hours.
100 simultaneous connections (pool 20 per instance x 5 instances).

The DB pool (20 per instance) limits database concurrency, but it does not bound Celery worker memory. So the unbounded Celery memory issue during large portfolio recalculation is not solved by pool sizing alone.
No. Fargate Spot is restricted to batch workloads only (for example portfolio-recalc and digest) and not always-on services like Auth because of interruption risk.
The system doctrine is stateless JWT auth validated via JWKS across gateway and services (all-services-auth); this discourages instance-local in-memory session state.
30-day backups provide retention for restore points, while DR targets require replication to us-west-2 with RPO 1 hour and RTO 4 hours; they are complementary controls.
The RDS upgrade required using sslmode=verify-full and the global-bundle.pem CA bundle in the DB connection configuration.
id, account_id, security_id, acquisition_date, cost_basis, quantity, adjusted, created_at.
After password verification in login, MFA is enforced if enabled: user must complete TOTP (or recovery code flow) before tokens are issued.
10 recovery codes are generated; each is single-use and permanently consumed when used.
Exponential backoff with 5 attempts within 24 hours.

At rest: passwords are stored as Argon2id hashes (64MB/3 iterations/4 parallelism) and PII uses AES-256-GCM with per-tenant keys. In transit: service-to-service traffic uses mTLS via Envoy, DB connections use sslmode=verify-full with global-bundle.pem, JWTs use RS256 with JWKS-based verification, and secrets rotate every 90 days.

Login with MFA flow: request enters API Gateway (schema validation), Lambda authorizer performs JWKS-based JWT validation/claim injection where applicable, traffic goes via VPC Link to ALB (1000/min/IP), reaches auth handlePostLogin, password is verified with Argon2id, MFA challenge is required when enabled (TOTP or recovery code), then RS256 tokens are issued (access 15 minutes, refresh 7 days rotate-on-use).

Billing Service should follow existing platform patterns: JWT validation via JWKS (and gateway Lambda authorizer path), ECS Fargate deployment pipeline (GitHub Actions: lint->typecheck->test->build->ECR->ECS), App Mesh/Envoy mTLS, RFC7807 errors with traceId, real-PG testcontainers (no DB mocks), naming pattern handle{Verb}{Resource}, pool limit 20/instance, ALB/API gateway limits, and no Fargate Spot if it is always-on.

At 1500 req/min from one IP: API Gateway global throttle (5000 burst/2000 steady) is not the first bottleneck at that rate, Lambda authorizer still processes allowed requests, then ALB per-IP limit at 1000/min enforces cutoff so about 500 req/min are throttled before containers; up to 1000 req/min proceed to auth handlers.

Dependencies on JWT signing material: Auth Service depends on the private signing key to issue RS256 tokens; JWKS endpoint exposes verification keys; gateway Lambda authorizer depends on JWKS for validation; all services (including Portfolio and Notification) depend on JWKS for token validation.

Pipeline: code changes go through Claude Code review before merge, then GitHub Actions runs lint->typecheck->test->build, publishes image to ECR, deploys to ECS on Fargate, and production rollout uses blue-green ALB target switching for zero downtime; Fargate Spot is used only for batch workloads.
